author: @kilokilos
text: Well I am at version 2 wind generator and is busy with the generator part. Everything is not as they seem. We have great talk with low wind speed. The challange is to make it work at low wind speeds. It has two sets of 5 blades made from two 55 gallon drums , horizontally mounted. It turns no matter what the wind direction. I will be happy with 500 watts at low speeds. At the coast this thing is a monster. 1 kW no problem. All this generation input is of great help. Soon we will own the world - lol

author: @didierdesbordesartisan
text:
Hi Bro! Thanks Always for your Always Awesome Ideas :)

By the way, a datum that could be Helpful for You:

- a Professional Cyclist can generate around 750 W by using both Feet and Hands Pedals, for few minutes.

It should give You a kind of Limite and Base for Calculation of the Relative// Efficiency of the Apparatus You Built.

For example: 50 W reached would mean 50 W / 750 W = 7% ;)

Be Well,
Kind Regards,
Didier

author: @gravelydon7072
text: Depending on what you can get it to drive at, a washing machine motor might work for you. I have test data on a Samsung 36 Pole that I rewired and at as low as 300RPM on the motor you would get 200+ Watts. If you could turn it up to 750RPM, you would see 600+ Watts which should not be possible for most people. 400RPM would get you 300 Watts which is about what most people could power.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the power doesn't come from the motor mate - it comes from your legs - the counter torque would limit this at around 200 watts or so for most people and 300 watts for an athlete - that is because that is what they have in their legs to overcome a motor's counter torque - it is really almost independent of the motor you use - the only real contribution a different motor would make is in terms of efficiency - a stepper is around 90% efficient if a washing machine motor is more efficient than that then it would help for sure - you just couldn't turn it at 750 rpm to get 600 watts - no one has that strength

author: @68HC060
text:
I love when you torque about gears and stepper motors. ;)
-I think may be a good idea to stay below the maximum RPM of the motor (which for many stepper motors are between 2000 and 4000 RPM).
Some people like running to stay in shape, but running does more harm than good, when compared to walking.
If one can combine walking with creating energy, that's a real good combination; the 'product' will encourage one to stay in shape.

author: @Killianwsh
text: Great build Rob!  I  wonder if this could be made more efficient by directly replacing the platform & belt on a conventional treadmill with  your human hamster wheel?   You could  rest the wheel directly on the drive rollers already built into the treadmill frame which are already coupled to a respectable sized dc motor (usually 3/4 to 3 hp depending on the model), previously used to drive the walking belt. I don't know if any of the electronics from could be reused, but given how the rollers are set up inside them i think it would possibly save a huge amount of time for anybody trying to copy this idea.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think it would make it more efficient per se - but it would make it easier to build and I do like the idea - a lot - cheers

author: @kreynolds1123
text: An average person might have difficulty putting out 300 watts or more for an extended period of time like an hour or there abouts. Obviously trained athletes can do better.

replies.author: @satibel
replies.text:
Even for athletes, over 300w is pretty hard, on a bike no one did over 400w for an hour, and iirc the record barely outputs over 300w.
That is to say, even for trained athletes, 300w over an hour is hard to achieve, especially on a wheel vs a bike.

On 95% low for 20 minutes, 30% of measured male cyclists have an average over 300w, and 1-2% over 400w, but there's a sample bias.

As a quick rule of thumb running is about 5 times less efficient as cycling, so even if someone manages 500w on a bicycle, you'd expect about 100w over an hour to be the maximum for a wheel like that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: on a machine like this weight is doing a lot of the work - all the person is doing is moving the weight - which happens to be their weight obviously - but it is really a weight driven machine as opposed to a muscle driven machine

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  in a giant  hamster wheel, a man has to walk uphill inside the wheel, essentially working against gravity very much like an inclined treadmill.

author: @jeffreyrood8755
text:
Great video and great build. 
It would work great if you made an Axial Flux type generator where you didn't have drag. Is this correct?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate the motor doesn't matter as much as folks seem to think - it's not about drag it's about counter torque - efficiency of the motor matters - of course - an average brushed dc is going to be around 70% or so in efficiency the hoverboard motor is around 80% and the stepper motor around 90% - I think axial flux motors are around 93% or so - so you would be gaining 3% or so
